schema.title: scenario / command
schema.anomaly_classes: subset of {compaction, export, flush, full_cpu, full_memory, network_bandwidth2}
schema.iotdb_subsystems: subset of {Flush, Compaction, MemTable, TsFile, WAL, Memory, Network, Compression, Query, Cache, Procedure, Schema, GC, Election}

tuples.title: Horizontal scaling: add DataNode to relieve cluster bottleneck
tuples.symptom: Sustained CPU/memory/disk/network saturation across all existing DataNodes; throughput plateaus despite client retry pressure
tuples.root_cause: Workload exceeds the throughput capacity of current DataRegionGroups; data partitions are fixed and existing nodes are saturated
fix: Provision new node with identical OS/IoTDB version and free ports 6667/10730/10740/10750/10760/9090/9190/3000
Configure dn_seed_config_node = <primary ConfigNode internal address:port>
Start with sbin/start-datanode.sh -d, verify with SHOW DATANODES (status Running)
Note: only NEW data automatically lands on new nodes; existing data remains until manual region migration

tuples.title: MIGRATE REGION — rebalance hot DataRegion after scaling
tuples.symptom: After adding nodes, old nodes remain hot while new nodes are idle; per-node disk usage skewed; per-node CPU/load skewed
tuples.root_cause: IoTDB only auto-balances new data created after Thursday 08:00 reschedule; historical regions stay where they are
fix: SHOW REGIONS — identify hot region IDs and their current DataNodes
MIGRATE REGION <regionId> FROM <srcDataNodeId> TO <dstDataNodeId>
Region goes through Adding (copy data to dst) → Removing (delete data from src) states
Migration time ≈ region data size ÷ network transfer rate (e.g. 1TB at 100MB/s ≈ 3 hr)
Migrate one region at a time to avoid network/CPU stampede

tuples.title: REMOVE / re-add CONFIGNODE — keep ConfigNode quorum at 1 or 3
tuples.symptom: ConfigNode count is 2 (no HA) or >3 (performance degradation); ConfigNode-leader election unstable; cluster meta operations slow
tuples.root_cause: ConfigNode Ratis group requires odd-numbered membership for clean majority; 2 nodes cannot tolerate a single failure
fix: SHOW CONFIGNODES — list NodeIDs and roles
REMOVE CONFIGNODE <id>  — drop excess or faulty node
Start replacement via sbin/start-confignode.sh on a new host
Verify final count is exactly 1 (single-node) or 3 (HA cluster)

tuples.title: START / STOP REPAIR DATA — fix bad TsFiles with out-of-order data
tuples.symptom: Logs show sequential-space files containing out-of-order timestamps; cross-space compaction fails; query results diverge across replicas
tuples.root_cause: Sequential TsFiles got corrupted (crash before flush completion, clock skew on insert) and now violate sequentiality invariant; compaction cannot proceed
fix: START REPAIR DATA [ON LOCAL|CLUSTER] — scans all pre-existing TsFiles and repairs bad ones in background
STOP REPAIR DATA to pause; START again to resume from checkpoint
Watch compaction dashboard: seq/unseq counts should converge as repair progresses
Best run during low-traffic window — repair competes with normal compaction for IO

tuples.title: EXPLAIN — preview distribution plan to diagnose query design issues
tuples.symptom: Slow query but unclear which operator is expensive; want to validate filter pushdown / partition routing before running
tuples.root_cause: Some queries fan out across many DataRegions (poor partition pruning); ORDER BY / GROUP BY may force sort with insufficient memory
fix: EXPLAIN <SELECT_STATEMENT> — returns tree of operators (SeriesScan, FullOuterTimeJoin, Sort, etc.) and per-fragment partition assignment
Look for: many SeriesScan-N nodes (wide fan-out), missing time filter (no Partition narrowing), giant Sort/Aggregate nodes
Rewrite query: add time filter, use slimit, align by device, or reduce metric count

tuples.title: EXPLAIN ANALYZE [VERBOSE] — profile actual query execution with per-operator costs
tuples.symptom: Need to know exact CPU/IO breakdown of a slow query; need TsFile-level scan counts; query timed out and need post-mortem
tuples.root_cause: Without runtime statistics, cannot isolate whether bottleneck is in SeriesScan (file IO/cache), Sort (memory), TimeJoin (cross-region) etc.
fix: EXPLAIN ANALYZE <SELECT_STATEMENT>  — runs the query and collects per-PlanNode stats (CPU Time, output rows, memory)
Add VERBOSE for full operator detail
Inspect: Total Wall Time, Seq/UnSeq File counts (compaction state), CPU Time per PlanNode (find bottleneck node)
If query timed out, read logs/log_explain_analyze.log — Explain Analyze writes interim snapshots so even timeouts are debuggable
Tune merge_threshold_of_explain_analyze (hot-reloadable) when many similar nodes flood output

tuples.title: Detect compaction-induced slow reads via EXPLAIN ANALYZE
tuples.symptom: Specific query has High Seq File(closed) count or unusually large UnSeq File count in EXPLAIN ANALYZE output; per-fragment Wall Time spent in initDataQuerySource
tuples.root_cause: Many small TsFiles → many SeriesScan instances → slow file open / metadata load; symptom of compaction backlog
fix: If EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows e.g. 'Seq File(closed): 50+' for one Fragment, that DataRegion has compaction lag
Cross-check Storage Engine dashboard file_count metric
Trigger START REPAIR DATA if out-of-order; otherwise wait for compaction or raise concurrent_compaction_thread
